md: ### Four points on a circle

A **cyclic quadrilateral** is a quadrilateral whose four vertices all lie on a single circle. Not every quadrilateral is cyclic — most quadrilaterals you draw at random *can't* be inscribed in a circle.
But when a quadrilateral *is* cyclic, a beautiful property holds:
**Opposite angles of a cyclic quadrilateral are supplementary (sum to 180°).**
Drag the four red points around the circle. The opposite angle sums (shown in the readout) stay locked at $180°$ — that's the cyclic quadrilateral theorem in action.

blocks.title: Why it works
blocks.body:
Take cyclic quadrilateral $ABCD$. Angle $\angle A$ is an inscribed angle subtending arc $BCD$ (the arc from $B$ to $D$ that does NOT contain $A$). Angle $\angle C$ is an inscribed angle subtending arc $DAB$ — the *other* arc from $B$ to $D$.

The two arcs together make the entire circle: $360°$. By the Inscribed Angle Theorem, $\angle A = \tfrac{1}{2}\,(\text{arc } BCD)$ and $\angle C = \tfrac{1}{2}\,(\text{arc } DAB)$. Summing:

$\angle A + \angle C = \tfrac{1}{2}\,(\text{arc } BCD + \text{arc } DAB) = \tfrac{1}{2}\,(360°) = 180°.\;\blacksquare$

md: ### The converse — a tool for spotting cyclic quadrilaterals
**If opposite angles of a quadrilateral sum to 180°, the quadrilateral is cyclic.**
This converse is enormously useful in olympiad geometry. To prove four points lie on a circle, show that one pair of opposite angles in the quadrilateral they form is supplementary.
### Worked example
Quadrilateral $ABCD$ is cyclic. $\angle A = 80°$ and $\angle B = 95°$. Find $\angle C$ and $\angle D$.
**Opposite angles supplementary:** $\angle A + \angle C = 180°$ $\Rightarrow$ $\angle C = 100°$.
$\angle B + \angle D = 180°$ $\Rightarrow$ $\angle D = 85°$.
**Check:** $80 + 95 + 100 + 85 = 360°$ ✓ (any quadrilateral has angle sum $360°$).

blocks.title: Ptolemy's Theorem (bonus)
blocks.body:
For a cyclic quadrilateral $ABCD$, the product of the diagonals equals the sum of products of opposite sides:

$$AC \cdot BD = AB \cdot CD + AD \cdot BC.$$

This is a deeper cyclic-quadrilateral result, used to derive trigonometric identities like the sine/cosine sum formulas. Worth knowing exists, even if you don't use it in school geometry.

md: ### Which quadrilaterals are always cyclic?
**Squares** — yes. All angles are $90°$, so opposite pairs sum to $180°$.
**Rectangles** — yes. All angles are $90°$.
**Isosceles trapezoids** — yes. Base angles are equal; opposite angles are supplementary.

**Parallelograms** (non-rectangle) — generally no. Adjacent angles are supplementary, but opposite angles are equal, so for opposite angles to sum to $180°$ they'd each have to be $90°$ — i.e., the parallelogram is a rectangle.
**Rhombi** (non-square) — no, same reason.
**General quadrilaterals** — almost never. You have to be lucky.
